skill.name: github-workflow-automation
skill.description: Guides GitHub Actions automation with swarm-based CI/CD templates, GitHub CLI workflows, release coordination, and repository management patterns.
skill.summary: Build and optimize GitHub Actions workflows with swarm-style CI/CD coordination.
skill.author: ruvnet

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many command-execution patterns, but they are Markdown examples for GitHub Actions, GitHub CLI, and npm-based tooling rather than auto-running skill code. No prompt injection attempt or confirmed malicious intent was found. The skill still carries medium operational risk because following the guidance can execute remote packages, change workflows, create issues or pull requests, and pass secrets to external commands.

medium_findings.title: Side-effectful External Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill provides many examples that run GitHub CLI commands and npm packages. This is appropriate for a GitHub automation skill, but execution can modify repositories, pull requests, issues, releases, and CI workflows when copied or followed by an agent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in Markdown examples and match the skill purpose. The risk is operational rather than confirmed malicious because they require user or agent execution.

medium_findings.title: Automated Workflow and Deployment Changes
medium_findings.description: Several examples encourage automatic fixes, generated workflows, and deployment execution. These actions can create files, open pull requests, or run deployments without enough human review if used with broad permissions.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples explicitly describe automatic fixing, automatic deployment strategy execution, and workflow file creation. Legitimate use is plausible, but these are sensitive repository operations.

medium_findings.title: Secrets Passed to External Tooling
medium_findings.description: The workflow guidance shows secret-derived configuration and an API key made available to a command. This is a normal GitHub Actions pattern, but it increases impact if the invoked package or logs mishandle secret values.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variables come from GitHub secrets and are passed to command-line tooling. I found no evidence of exfiltration, so this remains a caution rather than a high-severity finding.

low_findings.title: False Positive Sensitive File and Weak Crypto Alerts
low_findings.description: The static SAM database and weak cryptography alerts appear to come from ordinary workflow text, variable names, or metadata. No evidence found of Windows SAM access or cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are documentation and workflow examples. They do not access host credential stores or implement cryptographic algorithms.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Documentation URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to GitHub CLI, GitHub Actions, Claude-Flow, and Ruv-Swarm documentation or repositories. They do not show data transmission by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are a support and documentation link list. No network call, payload, or credential transfer is present there.

low_findings.title: Hidden Home Directory Cache Paths Are Benign Examples
low_findings.description: The hidden file findings reference npm cache paths in GitHub Actions cache examples. This is expected CI cache configuration, not local secret file access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The paths are inside workflow cache examples and reference npm dependency caches. I found no evidence of reading hidden credential files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote Package Execution in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs users to invoke npm-distributed tools through command examples. This should be run only in trusted repositories and pinned where possible.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly invoke npm tooling. The pattern is legitimate for this skill but carries supply-chain and side-effect risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: Automatic Repository Mutation Flags
dangerous_patterns.description: Some examples include automatic fixing, generated workflow output, and automatic deployment execution. These patterns should require explicit user approval before use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited examples perform or describe repository-changing automation. I did not find malicious intent, but the pattern has meaningful impact if over-authorized.

dangerous_patterns.title: Secret-Backed Environment Variables Used by Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a workflow example where secret-backed values are exposed to a command environment. Users should restrict permissions and avoid untrusted packages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The use of GitHub secrets is explicit and semantically normal. Risk depends on the trustworthiness of the command receiving the values.

content.user_title: Automate GitHub Workflows with Swarm CI/CD
content.value_statement: GitHub Actions workflows can become slow, fragile, and hard to coordinate across repositories. This skill provides templates and command patterns for CI/CD, reviews, releases, security checks, and workflow optimization.
actual_capabilities: Provides GitHub Actions templates for CI, security scanning, PR validation, releases, and deployments.
Shows GitHub CLI and ruv-swarm command patterns for workflow generation, analysis, and optimization.
Describes Claude-Flow coordination modes for PR management, issue tracking, releases, and CI orchestration.
Covers workflow caching, timeouts, dependency ordering, OIDC authentication, and least-privilege permissions.
Includes troubleshooting examples for failed runs, log analysis, performance profiling, and workflow analytics.
limitations: Requires external tools such as gh, git, Node.js, claude-flow, and ruv-swarm.
Examples are templates and must be reviewed before use in production repositories.
Commands may create issues, pull requests, workflows, or deployments when run with write permissions.
The skill is documentation-only and does not include tested helper scripts.

use_cases.title: Create a CI Workflow
use_cases.description: Generate a starter GitHub Actions workflow with language detection, caching, tests, and basic failure analysis.

use_cases.title: Coordinate Pull Request Checks
use_cases.description: Plan automated linting, testing, security review, documentation checks, and PR comments for a review workflow.

use_cases.title: Standardize Release Automation
use_cases.description: Design release, deployment, rollback, and monitoring workflows across one or more repositories.

prompt_templates.title: Generate a Starter Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a repository without CI.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a basic GitHub Actions CI workflow for my repository. Include build, test, caching, and failure analysis steps.

prompt_templates.title: Improve an Existing Workflow
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate optimization of a current CI file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my current GitHub Actions workflow and suggest safer caching, parallel jobs, timeouts, and permissions. Explain each recommended change.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Pull Request Automation
prompt_templates.scenario: Team workflow for review quality and speed.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a PR validation plan using linting, tests, security checks, documentation review, and GitHub comments. Include approval gates.

prompt_templates.title: Design Multi-Repository Release Flow
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design a multi-repository release workflow with version alignment, changelog generation, staged deployment, and rollback controls.

output_examples.input: A developer asks for CI setup for a Node.js project.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns a GitHub Actions workflow plan with install, cache, lint, test, build, and failure-analysis stages.

output_examples.input: A team lead wants better pull request checks.
output_examples.output: The assistant proposes parallel validation agents, required status checks, security review, documentation review, and a concise PR comment summary.

output_examples.input: A DevOps engineer requests release automation guidance.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines tag-triggered release notes, artifact creation, deployment strategy selection, monitoring, and rollback review points.

best_practices: Review every generated workflow before committing it to a repository.
Use least-privilege GitHub token permissions and require approval for deployment jobs.
Pin third-party actions and packages where possible, especially in production workflows.
anti_patterns: Do not run automatic fixes or deployments without explicit human approval.
Do not pass repository secrets to untrusted packages or verbose logging commands.
Do not grant broad write permissions to workflows that only need read access.

faq.question: What does this skill help automate?
faq.answer: It helps design GitHub Actions workflows for CI, PR validation, releases, deployments, security checks, and workflow optimization.

faq.question: Does it execute commands by itself?
faq.answer: No. It is a documentation skill with command examples. Commands only run if a user or agent chooses to execute them.

faq.question: Which tools are required?
faq.answer: The skill expects GitHub CLI, git, Node.js, claude-flow, and ruv-swarm for the documented examples.

faq.question: Can it modify my repository?
faq.answer: Yes, if you run the examples with write permissions. Some examples create workflows, issues, pull requests, or deployment actions.

faq.question: Is it safe for production repositories?
faq.answer: Use it with review gates. Validate generated workflows, restrict permissions, and avoid automatic deployment without approval.

faq.question: Does it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
